title: Dr. Frank B. Hu - Gastronomy and Beyond Lecture Series - YouTube
text:
Indeed, it's such an honor to have
professor and chair Frank who he he is
from the department of nutrition Harvard
Thchan School of Public Health.
professor of medicine and um professor I
really love the way you look at food in
a different way. So the title of this
talk is ancient wisdom meets modern
science the Mediterranean diet. Um thank
you very much indeed for joining us.
Thank you very much indeed for your
time. Thank you very much indeed for
sharing your knowledge. So uh ready when
you are.
>> Yeah sure. Thank you Adana for inviting
me and um uh I have been on the faculty
of Harvard uh TH public health for more
than 20 years. I've been uh uh doing
nutrition research for more than 30
years. Um and uh um much of uh uh
nutrition research has been focused on
uh the health benefits of uh
Mediterranean diet. uh even though I
grew up in China and I have uh of course
um uh uh enjoying uh traditional Asian
diet uh and uh uh our research has also
um discovered many health benefits of
the traditional Mediterranean diet. So
uh what uh we are doing in many ways is
to um blend or combine uh different
elements of the traditional Asian diet,
traditional Mediterranean diet and uh
create some kind of hybrid or fusion
diet that take the uh combines the best
of the west and east uh dietary uh
traditions. So today today I'm going to
talk about uh the the the science that
we have uh um worked on in terms of uh
various dietary patterns especially the
Mediterranean diet uh in terms of
cardiovascular disease prevention uh uh
longevity and also uh uh environmental
sustainability.
So uh before I talk about the science I
just want to uh uh quickly uh mention
that we are facing global uh chronic
disease epi ep epidemic. Um so uh
chronic disease or NCDS like
cardiovascular disease, diabetes and
cancer account for uh more than 70% of
global deaths and uh those diseases are
rising very rapidly in low and middle
inome countries. We know that uh the
major drivers of NCDS uh include
unhealthy diet, physical inactivity,
tobacco use and also aging populations
and NCDs um impose huge economic cost uh
straining uh health systems uh worldwide
and uh preventions through diet and
lifestyle is key to reducing global uh
chronic disease burden.
And when we study the relationship
between diet and chronic diseases, uh
the um uh the strongest study design or
the gold standard uh would be randomized
clinical trials. Uh suppose you want to
study uh red meat consumption and uh
risk of cancer. Uh ideally you would
randomize uh tens thousands of people to
a high red meat diet and a low red meat
diet and then uh after several years or
or even decades compare uh the incidence
of uh certain cancers between the two
groups and then uh uh make a causal
inference about whether reducing red
meat uh is beneficial for uh preventing
cancer. So this kind of study design
although ideal um is typically not
feasible because of high cost low
compliance and sometimes issues. So um
that's why in most situations uh we have
to rely on uh large obser observational
cohort studies. Uh in this type of
studies we don't randomize people to
different diets. Instead we observe uh
the incidence of heart disease or cancer
uh among people who uh are eating
different diets uh for example high red
meat diet or low red meat diet for uh
years or decades and then to see whether
um red meat consumption is associated
with uh uh disease risk. So uh in this
kind of studies um we uh don't need to
randomize people uh into different
diets. Uh but um there is a issue with
uh confounding meaning that people eat
high red meat versus people who eat low
red meat. they are not different uh in
meat intake but also different in uh
physical activity in um soio economical
status. So we need to um uh take into
account those so-called confounding
factors when we evaluate uh the
relationship between uh meat intake and
disease outcomes. So in this type of
studies um even if uh we uh can adjust
for large number of confounding factors
uh we still cannot rule out uh the
so-called and measured confounding or
residual confounding or residual
confounding.
uh in the past several decades we have
been conducting several large cohort
studies at Harvard um uh including the
nurses health study which was
established um in 1976
uh almost 50 years ago. So this year is
a important milestone for the nurses uh
health study and in those cohorts we
have collected detailed data on diet,
lifestyle, uh biomarkers um and health
conditions and uh those cohorts uh have
provided a unique opportunity for us to
evaluate the long-term relationship
between certain uh dietary habits and uh
uh risk of uh diabetes, cardiovascular
disease, cancer and other chronic
diseases.
So, uh in the last several decades, uh
the nurses health study cohorts uh
together with other large cohort studies
have made major impact on nutrition
research, clinical practice and public
health guidance. Uh we have published
thousands of papers uh through uh the uh
data collected in the nurse's cohorts
and uh uh those uh studies have uh uh
transformed uh many uh nutrition
recommendations or policies uh globally
and the some of the major findings from
our large cohort studies. Um three
almost three decades ago we discovered
uh that higher consump trans fat from
partially hydrogenated vegetable oils uh
was associated with increased risk of
cardiovascular disease and that led to
um the FDA uh ban on trans fat. So um
most of trans fat in the US uh has been
uh removed from the food supply. We also
found that the type of fats or quality
of fat uh quality of carbohydrate is
more important than total amount of fat
or carbohydrate in our diet. Uh we found
that um uh higher consumption of red and
processed meat is associated with uh
risk of cardiovascular disease and
cancer. And uh the good news is that um
uh moderate consumption of coffee uh is
found to be beneficial for the
prevention of uh chronic diseases
including diabetes, cardiovascular
disease and even some cancers.
And uh um in uh the last two decades we
have focused on uh holistic dietary
patterns in including the Mediterranean
dietary pattern, the planetary health
diet which is not just beneficial for
human health but also for environmental
sustainability.
uh we found that uh uh most of chronic
diseases like diabetes,
cardiovascifications.
Um we found that um obesity and weight
gain um are strongly associated with uh
chronic disease risk including uh
cardiovascular disease uh stroke uh many
cancers and also uh decreased likelihood
of healthy aging. And uh in the nurses
health study we found that a moderate
alcohol consumption is associated with
increased risk of breast cancer in those
response manner.
Um
three decades ago uh in the US very few
people have heard heard of the
Mediterranean diet but uh now
Mediterranean diet has become very uh
popular and uh in the last eight years
uh the Mediterranean diet was um ranked
as the overall best diet by the US news

incredible. It just means that
Mediterranean diet has become a
mainstream diet in the US has become

for the US population

in the same time consumption of olive
oil has increased dramatically in the US

increase become more and more popular uh
in in uh for US uh uh consumers.
And when we talk about Mediterranean
diet it's not just uh single uh diet
because many countries that border uh
the Mediterranean Sea. Uh so uh the
Mediterranean diet is a dietary pattern
in the olive oil uh growing region and
uh again there is no single
Mediterranean diet and the uh uh the
type of cuisine type of foods and uh uh
eating habits uh differ significantly
across different uh populations in the
Mediterranean uh region. However, they

coined the Mediterranean diet u is
actually the American scientist um and
so Keith and he um conducted uh I think
really a NMAK study uh uh nutrition
study in the 1950s 1960s it's called u
the seven countries study so he uh
compared um heart disease r uh uh
incidents across uh seven different
countries uh Greece, Italy, Leinance,
Japan, Yugoslavia, Finland and the US.
He found that uh um the incidence rates
of heart disease was significantly lower
in um uh Greece uh and also in Japan

found that uh some fats uh dietary fats
especially um the dietary fats from
olive oil uh was beneficial for uh

why he uh coined the term uh
Mediterranean diet to characterize
uh the dietary patterns in uh uh Greece
and other uh Mediterranean countries. He
postulated that uh those dietary habits
may uh be uh responsible for the lower
risk of cardiovascular disease in um
those populations.
And then in 1993,
our department uh together with the old
ways hosted the first uh international
conference on the Mediterranean diet and
can say answer keys uh here was one of

what will it in our department a

um I had the privilege to uh speak at

uh in the US.
So what is the Mediterranean diet? Um
and this is the um updated Mediterranean
diet pyramid. You can see that uh the
core uh dietary components include
higher intake of fruits and vegetables,
whole grains, nuts, legumes, olive oil
is the primary source of uh dietary fat
and moderate consumption of fish and

uh a very common uh feature of the
Mediterranean dietary pattern is um
shared meals and uh social eating.
And uh several years ago I had the

which cons was considered as the
birthplace of the Mediterranean diet and
um uh the um uh traditional creatin food
and meals uh are made from u uh locally
u uh sourced ingredients. They are
mostly homemade and very uh few ultra
processed ingredients and uh they are
not just delicious but also
environmentally sustainable
and um uh two years ago uh Netflix um

the blue zones. So the blue jones uh are
recognized for um very longevity uh
except except exceptional longevity in
uh five different regions of the world
uh which include uh Sardinia, Italy,
Okalawa, Japan uh Eolia uh uh Costa Rica
uh IA Greece and Normaninda California.
You can see that two of the blue zones
come from the Mediterranean um
countries.
And uh recently we published a paper to
uh review uh the history evolution and
the scientific advances of the uh the
Mediterranean diet. Uh this is part of
the um
of the event to commemorate the three uh
the 30-y year anniversary of the
Mediterranean diet pyramid.
And uh in this uh review um we describe
uh the history and the cultural
traditions of the Mediterranean diet and
also um the scientific advances to
support the health benefits of the
Mediterranean diet on prevention of
cardiovascular disease, diabetes and
other chronic disease. And we also um uh
describe um uh modern sciences uh to um
uh provide uh biological
uh uh to provide insight into the
biological mechanisms underlying the
health benefits of the Mediterranean
diet. And we also describe uh the
culture adaptation of Mediterranean diet
in other populations and also um the
health the benefits of the Mediterranean
diet u environmental sustainability and
planetary health.
And the strongest evidence uh to support
the health benefits Mediterranean diet
come from uh this uh nonmark study
called the predimeat trial. And this
trial was conducted in 11 clinical
centers in Spain. I um have had the
privilege to uh collaborate with our
Spanish colleagues for uh more than 20
years and uh um was um involved in the
praimat trial uh from the beginning. Uh
this is a a very big uh study with more
than 7,000 participants were randomized
who were randomized to three groups.
Mediterranean diet uh in um supplemented
with extra virgin olive oil,
Mediterranean diet supplement with uh uh
u mixed nuts including um almonds,
walnuts and hazelnuts and then a control
diet. Uh the intervention lasted for
five years. Uh the primary outcome
include um included heart attack and the
stroke. So those are hard clinical
endpoints. After five years
intervention, we found that the
Mediterranean diet groups had 30%
reduction in heart attack or stroke
compared to uh the control diet. And the
Mediterranean diet interventions also
reduced risk of other uh um outcomes
including atrial fibrillation
uh peripheral artery disease and even
breast cancer. So um women who were
randomized to uh the Mediterranean diet
plus extravirgin olive oil had more than
uh 60% reduction in breast cancer risk
compared to the control diet and uh
those findings were replicated in
another uh Spanish um study called uh
cardio prev uh trial. This trial was
conducted among um patients who already
had um a heart attack and uh in this
trial uh the Mediterranean diet
intervention uh also reduced um
cardiovascular outcomes uh including
heart recurrent heart attacks or
cardiovascular diets by nearly 30%
compared to uh um the control diet.
And the Mediterranean dietary
interventions also reduced risk of type
2 diabetes by almost 30%
uh in the original uh predimeat study
and also in the predimeat uh plus trial
which was published uh just recently
and we know that olive oil is a hallmark

it is known as the liquid gold since uh

uh besides um uh the traditional

And the traditional oklawa diets are

some of their eating habits are very

For example, older Okalawans uh consume
a diet consistent with my calorical
restriction meaning that they stop

polyphenos
uh and those polyphenos are chemical

anti-inflammatory
and antioxidant uh properties which can

physically smaller physiologically

the health and the longer health span
and there's some uh strong biological

those um uh okalawa traditional okalawa
uh foods. on um the aging pathways uh
including reduced incident incident
growth factors uh factor uh one or IGF-1

uh uh biological aging uh pathway like

uh um pathways like AMP PK or citrine
one uh or uh NAD uh pathways which can

oxidative stress and uh improve u
biological um aging.
Recently uh my colleagues in China

they do share many common components. um
they're both uh plan forward dietary

uh in recent years there there is a

um promote healthy uh gut microbiome.

uh nutrition recommendations.
And uh we also found that uh not all

um a highly processed plant-based diet

uh uh profiling technique, we um develop

because um typically we rely on

dietary compliance
and uh using uh the urinary uh samples
in the predime trial.

um uh are associated with decreased risk
